hero.headline: I find products I love — then build for them.
hero.subhead: CS student who ships end-to-end across web, mobile, desktop, and browser extensions. AI-first by default.

colonistCallout.headline: I built a Colonist Stats Tracker before applying. That is how I work.
colonistCallout.note: Unofficial, fan-made, local-only, and designed around actual player uncertainty.

home.projectsLead: I like products where the interface, parser, and workflow all have to survive actual use.
home.calloutHeadline: I care about the product enough to build before permission.
home.calloutBody: The Colonist tracker is a small example: a local extension that watches the game log, computes state, and turns ambiguity into UI instead of hiding it.

about.heading: I ship across surfaces.
paragraphs: I am a third-year NTNU CS student. I tend to start with products I already use, then build tools around the pain I can feel: a Colonist browser extension, a graduation-credit checker, a LINE support bot, Android notification tooling, and AI-assisted learning workflows.

My background is split between product, engineering, community, and writing. That makes me comfortable with small details: the copy inside a popup, the edge case in a parser, the support message after a bug, and the UX of uncertainty.

projectsPage.title: Small products, shipped end to end.
projectsPage.lead: Each project is listed with one defensible sentence, status, tags, links, and a real local screenshot or a clearly labeled placeholder.

colonistPage.disclaimerBody: Not affiliated with, endorsed by, or sponsored by Colonist or colonist.io. The extension collects and transmits no data.

why.title: A product where UI clarity changes the game.
paragraphs: Colonist is interesting because every product decision is felt by real players in real time: hidden information, trade timing, game-log readability, and table state all have to stay understandable under pressure.
That is the kind of product I like building for. The rules are concrete, the users are active, and the interface has to respect both strategy and uncertainty.

fit.title: Product taste
fit.body: Board-game systems, player-facing UX, and visible tradeoffs.

fit.title: Engineering surface
fit.body: JavaScript, browser extensions, parsers, SPA behavior, and local state.

fit.title: Work style
fit.body: AI-first, written reasoning, fast prototypes, and honest uncertainty.

proof.title: Colonist.io Stats Tracker
proof.lead: A pure content-script overlay that watches Colonist's game log and keeps analysis local.

items.title: Dice distribution
items.body: Live counts and percentages for sums 2-12, colored against fair-dice expectations.

items.title: Resource tracking
items.body: Per-player resource estimates from initial yields, rolls, builds, trades, discards, and Monopoly.

items.title: Unknown cards
items.body: Robber and knight steals move into a separate unknown pool until spending makes a type obvious.

items.title: SPA resilience
items.body: MutationObserver re-attaches to the game log after page and match navigation.

strengths.title: Builder habits Colonist can use.
strengths.lead: The work below maps to UI/UX taste, JavaScript, real-player product sense, and async collaboration.

related.title: Other shipped surfaces.
related.lead: Web, LINE, Android, and canvas projects that show end-to-end shipping beyond a single stack.

strengths.title: Browser extension UI
strengths.body: Manifest V3, content scripts, lightweight overlays, and state that lives beside a real product.

strengths.title: Parser-heavy product work
strengths.body: The Colonist tracker reads game-log events, handles trades, robber uncertainty, and SPA navigation.

strengths.title: Clear uncertainty
strengths.body: Hidden cards are not guessed away. The UI exposes an unknown pool so the player knows what is inferred.

strengths.title: AI-first shipping
strengths.body: I use Claude Code heavily, but keep source-of-truth logic testable and local where privacy matters.

strengths.title: Async communication
strengths.body: At Cooby I wrote technical docs, handled VIP tickets, and translated feature details into user-facing language.

strengths.title: Real users, real constraints
strengths.body: I have shipped production PWAs, LINE flows, Android utilities, and local dev tools around actual workflows.

highlights: Third-year B.Sc. student in Computer Science and Information Engineering at NTNU.
English: near-native; TOEFL 115, TOEIC 980 / IELTS 8.0 level.
Inventor of US10699576B1 / TWM578665U, a vehicle collision-warning patent later sold for NT$400k and cited by GM Cruise, Huawei, and Plus.ai.
Dcard official business partner and creator with roughly 2M+ monthly impressions.
Founded Nothing Taiwan Community; presented Nothing Phone 3(a) to 40+ media outlets.
Cooby Chief of Staff Intern; proposed onboarding fixes that reduced activation time by 30%.
